## Duration dilation and signal stretching

A redshift mechanism must also account for time-stretched signals. The observations do not present only shifted wavelengths. They present supernova light curves, pulse trains, and other extended emissions whose observed durations scale with redshift.

The translation-yield map pairs these effects. If redshift is

$$
1+z=\frac{\chi_e}{\chi_o},
$$

then the corresponding duration mapping is

$$
\Delta t_o=(1+z)\Delta t_e.
$$

The compact derivation tracks the interval associated with an emitted feature. Suppose an emitter produces that feature over an emitted duration $\Delta t_e$. During emission, the translation yield is $\chi_e$, so the feature corresponds to a propagation interval

$$
L=\chi_e c\Delta t_e.
$$

At observation, the same interval is read under the later yield value $\chi_o$. The observed duration is therefore

$$
\Delta t_o=\frac{L}{\chi_o c}.
$$

Substitution gives

$$
\Delta t_o
=\frac{\chi_e}{\chi_o}\Delta t_e
=(1+z)\Delta t_e.
$$

The same result can be stated through neighboring pulses. If two nearby pulses traverse the same propagation path, then schematically

$$
\int_{t_e}^{t_o}\chi(t)c\,dt=D,
$$

and

$$
\int_{t_e+\Delta t_e}^{t_o+\Delta t_o}\chi(t)c\,dt=D.
$$

Subtracting the two path conditions gives the endpoint comparison. In the slowly varying endpoint approximation,

$$
\chi_o\Delta t_o=\chi_e\Delta t_e,
$$

and again

$$
\Delta t_o=(1+z)\Delta t_e.
$$

This is the main reason the yield reading is stronger than a redshift slogan. Wavelength stretching and duration stretching are two consequences of the same comparison between emission and observation yield states.

Actual transient data still require source physics, selection effects, lensing, propagation effects, and distance kernels. The claim here is narrower: at the kinematic level, translation yield couples spectral stretching and signal-duration stretching.
